one_line_takeaway: MAScope reframes MAS defense as execution-aware provenance analysis -- fusing kernel- and application-layer telemetry into cross-agent semantic graphs, extracting sensitive entities via hierarchical constraint prompting (HSEC), and having a Supervisor LLM audit reconstructed trajectories against intent/data-flow/control-flow policies -- and shows this beats a same-policy but reconstruction-free ChatGPT-5.2 baseline by a wide margin (85.3% vs 21.9% node-level F1) on a self-built, OWASP-Top-10-aligned simulated attack dataset.
key_finding: HSEC substantially improves sensitive-entity extraction over the unconstrained baseline: for Gemini-3, overall F1 rises from 48.2% to 75.7% (precision 41.5%->73.6%, recall 57.4%->77.9%); for GPT-5.2, overall F1 rises from 49.4% to 76.8% (precision 40.8%->75.7%, recall 62.7%->77.9%) -- both a roughly 27-28 point absolute F1 gain. For attack detection, MAScope's node-level detection reaches overall precision 96.5%, recall 76.4%, F1 85.3% (TP=55, FP=2, FN=17 against GT=72), and path-level detection reaches precision 81.3%, recall 56.5%, F1 66.7% (TP=13, FP=3, FN=10 against GT=23). The VanillaGPT baseline (same policies, no semantic flow reconstruction) performs far worse at node-level: precision 23.1%, recall 20.8%, F1 21.9% (TP=15, FP=50, FN=57). A qualitative case study (Figure 4: phishing/PII-exfiltration chain -- injected email prompt -> Planning Agent -> Data Agent SQL read of staff database -> Internal Agent mass-emails stolen addresses) illustrates that MAScope traces the full causal chain and flags it as a data-confidentiality violation, whereas VanillaGPT evaluates each step in isolation (internal Gmail use, routine-looking DB query) and misses the compound attack.
core_contribution: The paper proposes MAScope, an execution-aware attack detection framework for LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) that shifts defense from static input guardrails to analysis of reconstructed cross-agent behavior. It has three modules: (1) a dual-layer Data Collection module that fuses application-layer structured logging (agent interactions, tool I/O) with kernel-layer telemetry (ETW, eBPF) into a unified semantic event stream; (2) a Semantic Extracting & Flow Reconstruction module that uses a Hierarchical Sensitive Entity Constraint (HSEC) mechanism to extract sensitive entities/operational primitives from unstructured logs under a taxonomy of five top-level categories (Identity & Privacy, Credential & Secrets, System & Infrastructure, Code & Payloads, Sensitive File), assigns each entity a sensitivity score combining a category-based prior with context cues (secret-like patterns, high entropy, sensitive paths, egress destinations), and ranks candidate provenance paths by an aggregated, time-decayed risk score; and (3) a Trajectory Scrutiny module where a Supervisor LLM (ChatGPT-5.2) audits the top-ranked reconstructed trajectories against three policies -- Intent Consistency (deviation from a structured, session-level intent specification derived from the user's request/plan), Data Flow Confidentiality (sensitive entities reaching untrusted external destinations), and Control Flow Integrity (source node privilege lower than target node privilege) -- producing a binary violation decision plus a cited evidence string.
threat_model: The paper targets LLM-based multi-agent systems (built on frameworks like LangGraph/AutoGen/MetaGPT) that rely on autonomous execution and unstructured natural-language inter-agent communication. The attacker is external or embeds malicious content in data the system ingests (e.g., a spoofed webpage, a phishing email with an injected prompt, a poisoned tool description in the agent supply chain), producing indirect prompt injection that hijacks agent goals and chains several seemingly-benign micro-operations across different agents/time steps into a multi-step, cross-agent compound attack (e.g., data exfiltration, privilege escalation, tool misuse). The paper explicitly frames its evaluation around the OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications (2026) attack surface, reproducing 10 of those attack types (labeled circled numbers 1 through 10 in the paper, e.g., Agent Goal Hijack, Tool Misuse & Exploitation, Identity & Privilege Abuse, Agentic Supply Chain Vulnerabilities, Unexpected Code Execution, Memory & Context Poisoning, Insecure Inter-Agent Communication, Cascading Failures, Human Agent Trust Exploitation, Rogue Agent) and composing them into 10 compound multi-stage attack scenarios across 5 simulated application domains (S1 Corporate Recruitment, S2 Automated Email Orchestration, S3 Collaborative Programming Copilots, S4 Conversational Knowledge Assistants, S5 Enterprise Database Infrastructures). The stated premise is that conventional input guardrails/sandboxing cannot detect these because individual events look benign in isolation and only the reconstructed multi-agent trajectory reveals malicious intent.
limitations: The paper's own text and results surface several limitations: (1) the evaluation dataset (14,927 records, 10 simulated scenarios) is synthetic/self-constructed via LangGraph simulations rather than drawn from real production MAS deployments, so generalization to real-world traffic/noise is untested; (2) path-level recall is markedly lower (56.5%) than node-level recall (76.4%), and the paper attributes some of this to a 'conservative' design choice where MAScope consolidates convergent attack paths into a single trajectory, explicitly trading recall for reduced false positives/noise; (3) both extraction and detection depend on proprietary frontier LLMs (ChatGPT-5.2, Gemini-3) as the entity extractor, privilege estimator, and Supervisor judge, so results are tied to those models' capabilities/costs and may not transfer to smaller/open models; (4) sensitivity scoring uses several hand-set weights/thresholds (base category weights b(c,sc), context-cue weights delta_k, temporal decay gamma, threshold tau_sens) whose sensitivity/robustness is not analyzed in the excerpted sections; (5) privilege levels for Control Flow Integrity are themselves estimated by a foundation model from contextual signals rather than ground-truth system permissions, introducing a potential source of estimation error into that policy; (6) no comparison against non-LLM structural/graph-based MAS defenses (e.g., the G-Safeguard topology-pruning approach cited in Related Work) or against formal-verification/pre-execution-constraint defenses is reported -- the only baseline is VanillaGPT, a weaker ablation of the authors' own pipeline rather than an independent prior system; (7) source code is hosted anonymously (anonymous.4open.science) suggesting a submission still under double-blind review, so external reproducibility could not be verified from the text alone.

datasets_used: Self-constructed dataset of 14,927 records from 10 high-fidelity LangGraph simulation scenarios across 5 application domains (S1 Corporate Recruitment Platforms, S2 Automated Email Orchestration Systems, S3 Collaborative Programming Copilots, S4 Conversational Knowledge Assistants, S5 Enterprise Database Infrastructures), manually labeled for sensitive entities, critical attack paths, and malicious nodes; attacks aligned with the OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications (2026)
quantitative_results: HSEC (Gemini-3) sensitive-entity extraction overall: F1 75.7% (Precision 73.6%, Recall 77.9%), up from unconstrained Gemini-3 baseline F1 48.2% (Precision 41.5%, Recall 57.4%)
HSEC (GPT-5.2) sensitive-entity extraction overall: F1 76.8% (Precision 75.7%, Recall 77.9%), up from unconstrained GPT-5.2 baseline F1 49.4% (Precision 40.8%, Recall 62.7%)
MAScope node-level attack detection: Precision 96.5%, Recall 76.4%, F1 85.3% (TP=55, FP=2, FN=17, GT=72)
MAScope path-level attack detection: Precision 81.3%, Recall 56.5%, F1 66.7% (TP=13, FP=3, FN=10, GT=23)
VanillaGPT baseline node-level attack detection: Precision 23.1%, Recall 20.8%, F1 21.9% (TP=15, FP=50, FN=57, GT=72)
baselines_compared: VanillaGPT: a standalone ChatGPT-5.2 instance prompted with the identical security detection policies as MAScope's Supervisor but without the semantic flow reconstruction pipeline, forced to judge alerts directly from raw execution logs
Ablation-style comparison of sensitive-entity extraction with vs. without HSEC (Hierarchical Sensitive Entity Constraint), on two backbone LLMs (Gemini-3 and GPT-5.2)
